principles.name: Single Responsibility Principle
principles.summary: A class or module should have one, and only one, reason to change.
principles.rationale: When a component has multiple responsibilities, changes to one concern risk breaking unrelated functionality. Isolating responsibilities makes components easier to understand, test, and modify independently.
principles.guidance: Ask: 'What is the single job of this class?' If you need the word 'and' to describe it, it has too many responsibilities. Extract the second concern into its own class. In Jarvis, each subsystem owns exactly one domain — the Loader loads, the Repository stores, the Service queries.
violations: A class that both parses JSON and queries a database
A service that handles business logic and also formats output for the UI
A function named process_and_save() or validate_and_send()
examples: AcademyLoader only reads and validates JSON — it does not query or cache.
AcademyService only provides lookups — it does not read files or manage persistence.
references: Robert C. Martin — Clean Code (Chapter 10)
Robert C. Martin — Agile Software Development: Principles, Patterns, and Practices

principles.name: Open/Closed Principle
principles.summary: Software entities should be open for extension but closed for modification.
principles.rationale: Modifying existing, tested code to add new behaviour risks introducing regressions. By designing extension points (abstract classes, interfaces, configuration), new functionality can be added without touching proven code.
principles.guidance: Define abstract base classes for extension points. New implementations extend the ABC rather than modifying the original. In Jarvis, AcademyRepository is the extension point — a new backend (SQLite, Redis) is added by implementing the ABC, not by editing JsonAcademyRepository.
violations: Adding an if/elif block to a function every time a new type is introduced
Directly modifying a stable class to add a new provider or format
Hardcoding format names as string literals inside switch-like logic
examples: AcademyRepository (ABC) is closed for modification. SqlAcademyRepository would extend it without touching existing code.
references: Bertrand Meyer — Object-Oriented Software Construction
Robert C. Martin — Agile Software Development

principles.name: Liskov Substitution Principle
principles.summary: Subtypes must be substitutable for their base types without altering the correctness of the program.
principles.rationale: If a subclass behaves differently from its declared contract, code written against the base class breaks unexpectedly. LSP ensures that polymorphism is safe and predictable.
principles.guidance: Any implementation of an ABC must honour the full contract — same return types, same exception semantics, same invariants. If a subclass cannot honour the contract, the hierarchy is wrong. In Jarvis, every AcademyRepository implementation must return the same model types and raise the same exceptions as documented on the ABC.
violations: A subclass overrides a method and returns a different type than the base declares
A subclass throws exceptions the base contract does not document
A subclass silently ignores a method the caller depends on
examples: JsonAcademyRepository.list_all() must always return List[EngineeringPrinciple] — never None, never a different model.
references: Barbara Liskov — Data Abstraction and Hierarchy (1987)
Robert C. Martin — Clean Architecture

principles.name: Interface Segregation Principle
principles.summary: Clients should not be forced to depend on interfaces they do not use.
principles.rationale: Fat interfaces force implementing classes to provide stubs for methods they do not need, creating maintenance burden and risk of misleading no-op implementations.
principles.guidance: Keep ABCs focused. If two groups of clients need different subsets of operations, define two smaller ABCs rather than one large one. In Jarvis, AcademyRepository only exposes read operations — a future write interface would be a separate ABC.
violations: An interface with 20 methods where most implementors only need 3
Implementing a method as 'raise NotImplementedError' to satisfy a bloated interface
Forcing a read-only client to import write methods it will never call
examples: AcademyRepository declares only 4 read methods. No write methods are present, so no implementor is forced to stub them.
references: Robert C. Martin — Agile Software Development

principles.name: Dependency Inversion Principle
principles.summary: High-level modules should not depend on low-level modules. Both should depend on abstractions.
principles.rationale: When high-level policy is coupled to low-level implementation details, changing the implementation forces changes to the policy layer. Depending on abstractions allows implementations to be swapped without affecting callers.
principles.guidance: Inject dependencies through constructors rather than instantiating them internally. AcademyService depends on AcademyRepository (the abstraction), not on JsonAcademyRepository (the implementation). Tests can inject a mock repository without touching the service.
violations: A service that calls JsonAcademyRepository() directly inside its own constructor
Hard-coding a file path inside a class that should receive it as a parameter
Importing a concrete class in a module that should only know about the abstract interface
examples: AcademyService.__init__(self, repository: AcademyRepository) — the service never knows which concrete repository it receives.
references: Robert C. Martin — Clean Architecture (Chapter 11)
Martin Fowler — Inversion of Control Containers and the Dependency Injection pattern

principles.name: Don't Repeat Yourself
principles.summary: Every piece of knowledge must have a single, unambiguous, authoritative representation in a system.
principles.rationale: When logic or data is duplicated, changes must be made in multiple places. Missing one creates inconsistency. DRY reduces the cost of change and eliminates an entire class of bugs caused by diverging copies.
principles.guidance: When you find yourself writing the same logic twice, extract it. When you find the same constant in two files, define it once. In Jarvis, REQUIRED_FIELDS is defined once in models.py and imported by loader.py — it is never duplicated.
violations: Copy-pasting a validation block into three different files
Defining the same constant in multiple modules
Writing similar SQL queries in multiple service methods instead of extracting a shared query builder
examples: REQUIRED_FIELDS tuple in models.py is the single source of truth for which fields a principle must have.
references: Andrew Hunt & David Thomas — The Pragmatic Programmer (Chapter 7)

principles.name: Keep It Simple, Stupid
principles.summary: Systems work best when they are kept simple. Avoid unnecessary complexity.
principles.rationale: Complexity is the root cause of most software failures. Simple systems are easier to understand, debug, extend, and hand to a new engineer. Complexity compounds — small unnecessary abstractions grow into unnavigable architectures.
principles.guidance: Choose the simplest solution that correctly satisfies the requirement. Do not add layers, patterns, or abstractions because they might be useful later. In Jarvis Sprint 001, the loader reads a file and returns a list — no caching, no connection pooling, no plugin system.
violations: Adding a factory, registry, and event bus to a system that has one data source
Writing a generic framework when a specific solution is all that is needed
Over-engineering a configuration system for a setting that never changes
examples: AcademyLoader.load() reads the file, validates it, and returns a list. Three clear steps. No indirection.
references: Kelly Johnson — Lockheed Skunk Works design principle (1960)
Robert C. Martin — Clean Code

principles.name: You Aren't Gonna Need It
principles.summary: Do not implement functionality until it is actually needed.
principles.rationale: Speculative code is code that must be maintained, tested, and understood — but may never be used. It increases cognitive load and often must be redesigned anyway when the real requirement arrives. YAGNI keeps the codebase lean and intentional.
principles.guidance: Implement only what the current sprint requires. If caching, search ranking, or AI integration might be needed in a future sprint, leave it out until that sprint. In Jarvis, Sprint 001 has no caching — because it is not needed yet.
violations: Adding a caching layer before any performance problem has been observed
Implementing plugin support before any second plugin exists
Writing serialisation logic for a format no consumer has requested
examples: JsonAcademyRepository loads data on construction with no caching — caching is YAGNI for Sprint 001.
references: Ron Jeffries — Extreme Programming Installed
Martin Fowler — Is Design Dead? (2000)

principles.name: Separation of Concerns
principles.summary: Different concerns of a system should be handled by different, isolated components.
principles.rationale: When concerns are mixed — for example, data access and business logic in the same class — changes to one concern leak into the other. Separation allows each concern to evolve independently.
principles.guidance: Identify the distinct concerns in your system (data loading, validation, querying, presentation) and give each its own module. The Engineering Academy separates: file I/O (Loader), data modelling (Models), storage contract (Repository), concrete storage (JsonRepository), and business queries (Service).
violations: A REST handler that queries a database, applies business rules, and formats HTML
A model class that also knows how to serialise itself to three different formats
A test file that also performs database migrations
examples: loader.py handles I/O and validation. service.py handles queries. Neither knows about the other.
references: Edsger Dijkstra — On the Role of Scientific Thought (1974)
Robert C. Martin — Clean Architecture

principles.name: Composition over Inheritance
principles.summary: Favour assembling behaviour from composable components rather than inheriting it from a parent class.
principles.rationale: Deep inheritance hierarchies are rigid — changing a parent class can unexpectedly affect all descendants. Composition assembles behaviour at runtime and allows it to be changed without restructuring the hierarchy.
principles.guidance: When a class needs a behaviour, inject a collaborator that provides it rather than inheriting it. AcademyService receives a repository via constructor injection — it does not inherit from a base service that embeds a repository.
violations: Inheriting from a 'BaseService' that hardcodes a specific repository type
Deep class hierarchies where leaf classes inherit irrelevant methods from ancestors
Using inheritance to share utility functions instead of extracting a utility module
examples: AcademyService composes with AcademyRepository. It can be given any repository implementation at construction time.
references: Gang of Four — Design Patterns: Elements of Reusable Object-Oriented Software (Chapter 1)
Joshua Bloch — Effective Java (Item 18)

principles.name: Single Source of Truth
principles.summary: Each piece of data or logic should have exactly one authoritative location.
principles.rationale: When the same data is stored in multiple places, the copies diverge over time. One gets updated, one does not. A single source of truth eliminates this class of inconsistency entirely.
principles.guidance: Define constants, configuration, and schema in one place and reference them everywhere else. In Jarvis, principles.json is the single source of truth for all engineering knowledge — the service, repository, and tests all read from it; none hardcode their own copies.
violations: Storing user settings in both a database and a config file
Duplicating environment configuration across multiple deployment scripts
Defining the same validation rule in the API layer and the database schema independently
examples: principles.json is the one authoritative record of all engineering principles. No other file duplicates them.
references: Andrew Hunt & David Thomas — The Pragmatic Programmer

principles.name: Fail Fast
principles.summary: Detect and report errors as early as possible, at the point closest to their cause.
principles.rationale: Errors that propagate silently are much harder to diagnose than errors that surface immediately. Failing fast gives a precise stack trace at the point of failure rather than a confusing downstream symptom.
principles.guidance: Validate inputs at system boundaries. Raise specific exceptions immediately when an invariant is violated. In Jarvis, AcademyLoader validates every field during loading — it does not allow an invalid principle to enter the system and fail later during a query.
violations: Returning None from a function instead of raising when data is missing
Catching all exceptions with a bare except and continuing silently
Deferring validation until a value is actually used, potentially many call frames later
examples: AcademyLoader._validate_record() raises InvalidPrincipleError immediately if a required field is missing — not when the missing field is first accessed.
references: Martin Fowler — Fail Fast (bliki, 2004)
Jim Shore — Fail Fast (IEEE Software, 2004)

principles.name: Law of Demeter
principles.summary: A method should only call methods on: itself, its parameters, objects it creates, and its direct fields.
principles.rationale: Reaching through object chains (a.b.c.do_something()) creates tight coupling between distant modules. If the middle object changes its internal structure, all callers that traverse it break.
principles.guidance: Each object should talk only to its immediate collaborators. If you find yourself writing long chains, extract the traversal into a method on the intermediate object. In Jarvis, AcademyService calls repository.filter_by_tag() — it does not reach into the repository's internal index directly.
violations: customer.getOrder().getLine().getProduct().getPrice()
Accessing service.repository._index directly from a test instead of through the public API
Traversing multiple object layers to extract a single value
examples: AcademyService calls self._repository.filter_by_tag(tag) — it does not access self._repository._index directly.
references: Karl Lieberherr & Ian Holland — Assuring Good Style for Object-Oriented Programs (1989)
Robert C. Martin — Clean Code (Chapter 6)

principles.name: Architecture Before Implementation
principles.summary: Design the full structure of a component before writing any implementation code.
principles.rationale: Implementation decisions made without a clear architectural plan lead to components that are difficult to integrate, test, and extend. Designing first surfaces integration questions before they become expensive refactoring.
principles.guidance: Write the sprint spec, define the module structure, define the public API, and identify all dependencies before writing the first line of production code. In Jarvis, every Genesis sprint begins with a complete spec document that is agreed upon before implementation starts.
violations: Writing code before agreeing on the module structure with the Chief Architect
Beginning implementation and discovering integration conflicts mid-sprint
Skipping the spec document and treating the first attempt as the design
examples: Genesis-019 Sprint 001 spec was fully written and approved before any code was produced.
references: Jarvis Engineering Constitution — Architecture Before Implementation

principles.name: Evidence Before Assumption
principles.summary: Base engineering decisions on observed evidence, not on assumptions about what might be true.
principles.rationale: Assumptions that turn out to be wrong cause failed fixes and wasted effort. Collecting evidence first — reading actual error messages, examining real data, running targeted checks — ensures the correct problem is being solved.
principles.guidance: Before diagnosing a failure, collect the error output, the relevant logs, and the actual state of the system. Do not guess at root causes. The Engineering Debugging subsystem (Genesis-017) implements this principle as the first step of every debugging cycle.
violations: Changing code based on a guess about what the error might be without reading the stack trace
Assuming a test fails for reason X and fixing X without confirming it is the cause
Making performance optimisations without measuring the actual bottleneck
examples: Genesis-017 Evidence Collection sprint gathers test output, logs, and git state before any analysis begins.
references: Jarvis Engineering Constitution — Evidence Before Assumption

principles.name: Capability Precedes Responsibility
principles.summary: A subsystem must fully demonstrate its capability before it is given more responsibility.
principles.rationale: Giving autonomous responsibility to an unproven system risks silent failures in production. Requiring demonstrated capability before expanded authority ensures each layer of the system earns its role.
principles.guidance: Build and freeze each capability layer before building the layer above it. The Engineering Academy must be complete and passing all tests before Engineering Workers are built to consult it. Workers must be stable before they are given autonomous engineering authority.
violations: Giving an Engineering Worker code-modification authority before its planning and testing subsystems are proven
Deploying an autonomous agent before its decision-making logic has been validated deterministically
Skipping test suites to ship a feature faster
examples: Genesis-019 (Academy) completes before Genesis-020 (Workers) begins. Workers cannot exist before the knowledge they consult exists.
references: Jarvis Engineering Constitution — Capability Precedes Responsibility

principles.name: Deterministic Behaviour
principles.summary: Given the same inputs, a Jarvis subsystem must always produce the same outputs.
principles.rationale: Non-deterministic systems are untestable and unpredictable in production. Determinism is the foundation of reliable automation — it is what makes it possible to freeze a milestone with confidence.
principles.guidance: Avoid random identifiers, timestamps, or unordered data structures in observable outputs. Sort results by a stable key. Use fixed seeds when randomness is unavoidable. In Jarvis, list_all() always returns principles sorted by id — the order is never dependent on insertion order or dict iteration order.
violations: Returning results in dict insertion order, which may differ between Python versions
Generating a UUID for a test fixture without a fixed seed
A search function that returns results in different orders on successive calls
examples: AcademyService.search() sorts results by id before returning — the order is always alphabetical regardless of which principles match.
references: Jarvis Engineering Constitution — Deterministic Behaviour

principles.name: Freeze Stable Milestones
principles.summary: When a sprint passes all tests and meets its definition of done, freeze it immediately.
principles.rationale: Continuing to modify a passing sprint introduces regression risk and makes it harder to identify when a failure was introduced. A frozen milestone is a known-good checkpoint the team can always return to.
principles.guidance: When all tests pass and the definition of done is met, commit with the Genesis freeze tag, push to GitHub, and create a ZIP backup. Do not add 'just one more thing' after the freeze. New work begins in a new sprint.
violations: Continuing to refactor a sprint after its tests already pass
Adding features to a frozen milestone instead of opening a new sprint
Pushing additional commits to a frozen sprint branch without incrementing the sprint number
examples: Genesis-018 Sprint 006 was frozen at commit a80f7ff with all 429 checks passing. No further changes were made to that sprint.
references: Jarvis Engineering Constitution — Freeze Stable Milestones

principles.name: One Responsibility per Subsystem
principles.summary: Each Jarvis subsystem owns exactly one domain. It does not cross into another subsystem's territory.
principles.rationale: Subsystems that take on multiple domains become coordination bottlenecks and create hidden coupling. Keeping one domain per subsystem means failures are localised and changes are contained.
principles.guidance: Name each subsystem after its single domain. If you cannot describe a subsystem in one noun (Academy, Loader, Dispatcher, Worker), it may have multiple responsibilities. In Jarvis, the Loader loads, the Repository stores, the Service queries — none of them do anything else.
violations: An Engineering Worker that also manages its own session state
A Repository that also performs business logic filtering
A Loader that also caches and invalidates data
examples: AcademyLoader: loads. AcademyRepository: stores. AcademyService: queries. Three classes, three responsibilities.
references: Jarvis Engineering Constitution — One Responsibility per Subsystem

principles.name: Local First When Practical
principles.summary: Prefer local, offline-capable solutions over network-dependent ones when they meet the requirement.
principles.rationale: Network dependencies introduce latency, cost, availability risk, and privacy exposure. When a local solution is sufficient, it is faster, cheaper, and more reliable.
principles.guidance: Engineering knowledge belongs in a local JSON file, not fetched from a remote API at runtime. Test runners execute locally. Debugging analysis runs locally. Network calls are introduced only when local capability is genuinely insufficient.
violations: Fetching engineering principles from a remote API when they could be bundled as data
Requiring internet connectivity to run the test suite
Storing local configuration in a cloud service when a config file would suffice
examples: principles.json ships with Jarvis as a local data file. No network call is ever made to retrieve engineering principles.
references: Jarvis Engineering Constitution — Local First When Practical

principles.name: Test Before Freeze
principles.summary: No sprint is frozen until all its tests pass deterministically.
principles.rationale: Freezing a failing sprint creates a broken baseline. Future sprints built on a broken baseline inherit its failures. A frozen sprint must be a provably good checkpoint.
principles.guidance: Run the full test suite before every freeze. If any test fails, the sprint is not done. Fix the failure, re-run, and only freeze when all checks pass. The Genesis archive records the passing check count at each freeze.
violations: Freezing a sprint with known failing tests and planning to fix them in the next sprint
Skipping the test run before a freeze because 'it was passing earlier'
Merging code that has not been tested locally
examples: Genesis-017 was frozen only after all 429 checks passed on the first complete run.
references: Jarvis Engineering Constitution — Test Before Freeze
